summary: Jeff Atwood highlights frustrating UI design patterns using examples from Lotus Notes and nVidia drivers. He criticizes Lotus Notes' nonsensical dialogs as beyond redemption, then pivots to praise an nVidia feature that explains why controls are disabled. The core complaint is that disabled UI elements typically offer no feedback about why they're unavailable, forcing users to randomly experiment to understand the interface's state.
key_points: Lotus Notes serves as an example of enterprise software with incomprehensible UI dialogs
Disabled/greyed-out controls are a major usability problem because they don't explain themselves
Good UI design should tell users WHY something is disabled, not just that it is
Users shouldn't have to randomly click around to understand interface modality
Enterprise software quality often stagnates for years without improvement
money_quotes: I've given up criticizing Lotus Notes. There's no point. It's like making fun of the mentally challenged.
I hate the way disabled controls give you no feedback as to why they are disabled.
Why can't you click on that greyed out menu? Why is that button greyed out? Who knows. You just can't.
You have to suss out the meaning behind the modality by randomly clicking on stuff to see what enables and disables. Ugh.
In case you were wondering: no, things haven't gotten any better since 1999.
key_insight: Disabled UI controls should always communicate why they're disabled rather than leaving users to guess through trial and error.
